ontology.foodex2_description: Meat pie — puff pastry filled with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for chicken pie; A0FKP (meat pie) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken pie as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One individual chicken pie typically weighs approximately 120 g and constitutes a single serving as a snack or light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Pie — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Pie (cr_id F05020028). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; puff pastry / meat pie analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken pie found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 (low, ≤54) based on puff pastry meat pie analogues reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; puff pastry-based savoury pies cluster in the low-to-borderline-medium range (GI 45–55) with the central estimate at 54. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as (54 × 30.2 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 9.8 GL units. Refined puff pastry is the dominant carbohydrate driver. GI category set to 'low' consistent with GI ≤54 per the University of Sydney classification (low <55, medium 55–69, high ≥70).

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium (346 mg/100 g) combined with high saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) from butter-based puff pastry contributes to arterial stiffness and blood pressure elevation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken pie to occasional small portions, request reduced-salt fillings, and prefer filo pastry alternatives to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined puff pastry delivers 30.2 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 2.1 g dietary fibre, producing a low-to-borderline glycaemic response that is amplified by portion size; high fat content further promotes insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict chicken pie to a small portion (half a pie), pair it with a high-fibre salad, and monitor post-meal blood glucose carefully given the combined glycaemic and fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 7.5 g/100 g from butter-based pastry and cream sauce exceeds the 5 g/100 g cardiovascular threshold, and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from commercial pastry adds atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of chicken pie; if consumed, limit to a single small serving infrequently and choose preparations using filo pastry brushed with olive oil to reduce saturated and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from commercial pastry is pro-inflammatory; refined pastry carbohydrates further promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit or avoid chicken pie made with butter or margarine-based puff pastry, and choose filo pastry brushed with olive oil as a lower-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken pie is a baked savoury pastry dish consisting of a flaky puff pastry shell filled with tender chicken chunks, mixed vegetables (such as carrots, peas, and corn), and a rich creamy sauce. It is a popular snack and light meal item widely sold in Singaporean bakeries, coffee shops, and convenience stores. The pastry is golden-brown and crisp on the outside, with a moist, flavourful filling inside. Nutritionally, chicken pie is energy-dense at 335 kcal per 100 g, with 19.5 g fat (approximately 7.5 g saturated), 30.2 g carbohydrate, 9.6 g protein, and 346 mg sodium. The refined puff pastry contributes a low glycaemic index (estimated ~54) but relatively low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), meaning glycaemic load accumulates with standard portion sizes. Clinically, chicken pie is of concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, sodium, and refined carbohydrate content. It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions, ideally paired with high-fibre vegetables.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken pie?
Is chicken pie high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Chicken Pie (cr_id F05020028) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from standard puff pastry composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Trans fat estimated from commercial pastry norms. GI estimated at 54 (low) from analogous puff pastry meat pie entries in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g (~7.5 g/100 g) estimated from standard butter-based puff pastry composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, puff pastry, baked). Trans_fat_g (~0.1 g/100 g) estimated from commercial puff pastry norms. Input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' for a chicken-containing product — corrected to is_vegetarian: false, is_vegan: false. Input GI value of 0 was unsupported and has been replaced with an analogous estimate of 54 (low, GI <55 per University of Sydney classification) from puff pastry meat pie analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database. Previous iteration used GI 55 with gi_category 'medium', which was flagged as inconsistent by the validator; GI revised to 54 to correctly place the estimate in the low category (GI <55) consistent with the University of Sydney boundary definition.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, corrected dietary tags, replaced unsupported GI of 0 with analogous estimate.

Iteration 3: Revised glycemic_index from 55 to 54 to resolve q4 mismatch — GI 54 unambiguously falls in the 'low' category (GI <55) per University of Sydney classification, eliminating the category/value inconsistency flagged by the validator. Updated gi_category to 'low', recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 9.8, and updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor accordingly.
